executive_summary: March 2026 paints a picture of a market where the two investor groups are pulling in strikingly different directions. Institutional investors went all-in on the belief that any dip would bounce back quickly — their Buy-on-Dips reading hit a perfect 100% — yet only a third of individual investors shared that view. Meanwhile, individual confidence in the market rising over the next year fell sharply, dropping to just 33%. Crash fears remain elevated for both groups, and neither side is especially convinced that stocks are fairly priced. The overall mood: professionals are betting on resilience while everyday investors are growing more cautious.

key_findings.headline: Individuals Turn Notably Pessimistic
key_findings.interpretation: Only a third of individual investors expect the market to be higher a year from now, down sharply from about 58% in February. Institutional investors held steady at 50% — a coin flip, suggesting neither conviction nor despair among the pros.

key_findings.headline: Crash Fears Linger On
key_findings.interpretation: Just 20% of individual investors feel confident a crash won't happen in the next six months, matching January's low. Institutional crash confidence dipped slightly from about 54% to 50%, meaning roughly half of professional managers still see a meaningful crash risk.

key_findings.headline: Pros And Public Diverge Sharply
key_findings.interpretation: Every institutional respondent in March said they expect a quick recovery from a one-day 3% drop — a striking level of unanimity. Individual investors moved the opposite way, falling from 81% to 40%, one of the widest gaps we've seen between the two groups.

key_findings.headline: Overvaluation Worries Persist Broadly
key_findings.interpretation: Half of institutional investors believe stocks are not overvalued, up from about 31% in February — a meaningful recovery. Individual investors slipped to 40%, suggesting most still see prices as stretched, though some who subscribe to market-efficiency views would argue the current price is the right price by definition.

detailed_analysis:
The standout number in March is that perfect 100% Buy-on-Dips reading from institutional investors. It's tempting to read this as pure bullishness, but it may reflect something narrower — professionals expressing faith in market plumbing and liquidity rather than in underlying fundamentals. Think of it less as "stocks are great" and more as "the market's shock absorbers still work." Individual investors clearly don't share that mechanical confidence, with their Buy-on-Dips score tumbling from 81% to 40%.

The 1-Year Outlook tells a cautious story on both sides. Individual investors' reading fell to 33%, the lowest of the three-month window. This could reflect anxiety about economic conditions, disappointment in recent returns, or simply the psychological weight of persistent crash fears. Institutional investors sitting at exactly 50% is itself notable — it's the definition of uncertainty, as if the professional community collectively shrugged.

Crash Confidence remains subdued across both groups. With only 20% of individuals feeling safe from a crash and just half of institutions feeling that way, it's fair to say that risk awareness is running high. Whether that worry is driven by geopolitical tensions, policy uncertainty, or simply the memory of past selloffs is hard to pin down from the survey alone.

Valuation Confidence offers a small bright spot for institutions, climbing from roughly 31% to 50%. One reading of this: professionals may have recalibrated their fair-value estimates upward, or recent price action may have made current levels look more defensible. It's also worth noting that for investors who believe markets are broadly efficient, the question of overvaluation is almost moot — prices reflect all available information, full stop. Individual investors remain more skeptical, with only 40% comfortable with current valuations.
market_context: [PLACEHOLDER: Add 2-3 sentences about recent market news relevant to this month's readings — e.g. Fed decisions, major earnings, economic data, or geopolitical events.]
methodology: These indices come from the Yale/Shiller U.S. Stock Market Confidence Surveys, which have tracked investor sentiment since 1989. Each month, roughly 100 wealthy individual investors and 100 institutional investment managers answer the same standardized questions. The consistency of those questions over 35+ years is what makes the data meaningful — you can actually compare investor psychology across very different market environments. The four indices each measure something distinct: • 1-Year Confidence: The share who expect prices to be higher in 12 months. • Crash Confidence: The share who believe a crash in the next 6 months is very unlikely (under 10% probability). A low reading means most people think a crash is plausible. • Buy-on-Dips Confidence: The share who believe that after a 3% single-day market drop, prices would fully recover within two days. • Valuation Confidence: The share who do not believe the market is overvalued. Surveys were conducted semi-annually before 2001 and have been monthly since July 2001. Respondents rotate over time, but consistent characteristics — high income, investment experience — are maintained across the panel.
